title: Appendix A — Topic
text:
Personally, I am a huge fan of chess and also video games and one of the main ways that they rank competitors in those two is through Elo ratings. This user guide will mainly focus on explaining how to build and also apply an Elo rating system for head-to-head competitions. This will explore how Elo rating measure different things like, competitor skill levels, how ratings change after match outcomes, and how rating difference can be used to predict future performance.
This guide will also include basic step-by-step instructions for implementing an Elo rating system in R using match outcome data and will also introduce a few helpful R packages. This guide will also explain when Elo rankings work well and also may not work as well compared to other ranking systems, like Glicko.
crumbs: Appendices
A  Topic

title: 1  User Guide: Implementing an Elo Rating System in R
text:
Introduction
Elo ratings are a popular way for ranking competitors in head-to-head competitions. Each competitor has a certain rating that updates after each and every match depending on the outcome. Rating differences can be used to predict future match results too. This guide explains how to implement an Elo rating system in R using the “PlayerRatings” package. It also includes instructions for loading and preparing match data, calculating Elo ratings, visualizing ratings and interpreting the results. The workflow is based on the concepts described by Glickman and Jones.(Glickman and Jones 2024)
Some things that users should have before starting is R and Rstudio installed and they should also be familiar with basic R operations such as loading datasets and using packages. The dataset should also include the following columns: player1, player2, and results. Matches should be in chronological order, and any results that are missing should be removed to make sure everything is accurate.
crumbs: 1  User Guide: Implementing an Elo Rating System in R

title: Appendix B — Needs Assessment
text:
What does someone trying to accomplish your chosen task need help with?

Understand how rating systems turn wins and losses into numbers
Formatting match data correctly in R
Choosing different settings like the K-factor in Elo
Running the model in R using different packages
Interpreting what the ratings actually mean

What parts are likely to be tricky?

Making sure the data is in the right format
Putting the matches in the correct time order
Understanding what rating differences mean
Checking if the model is predicting good enough

What resources are already available on this topic that may be helpful? Look for e.g. software vignettes, package documentation, papers about software packages, and so on.
Some R packages that help make this easier are BradleyTerry2, PlayerRatings, and elo. Each of these packages have help files in R, and also example workflows showing how to prepare data and run models. As for research papers, the annual review article is perfect.






Note



Make sure you do a good job of explaining how the models work and how they are fit – this is a tricky topic and you should absolutely not shy away from diagrams and cartoons and such to explain how things work. I find that those are usually far more effective than linking to yet another vignette (especially in this area).
A quick google pulls up things like this: https://researchonresearch.blog/2020/08/20/visual-demos-for-theoretical-results/ (I’m not familiar enough with the model to understand the pics, but hopefully you are?)
crumbs: Appendices
B  Needs Assessment

title: Appendix C — Task Analysis
text:
C.1 Additional Guidance
Here are some questions to guide you through the process of doing a task analysis. You don’t have to specifically answer each one of these (and some may not apply), but they should get you started thinking in the right direction.
What are the prerequisites, for both knowledge and e.g. software setup?
What are the basic requirements for any data that the method is used on? - Are missing values allowed? - Should the data be confined within a certain range? - Does the data have to be approximately normally distributed?
The data set should include player 1, player 2, match result, and maybe the date of the match. Missing results should be removed before running the model. Also we don;t need normal distribution since the outcome is usually win or loss.
What are the basic components of the task? -Outline these in a bit more detail
What decisions does the user need to be prepared to make on the fly?
What questions should the user ask of the “first draft” of the product? What adjustments may need to be made?
Your check-in should answer these basic questions (and similar concerns that apply more directly to your topic).
Once you’ve completed the check-in, you can use this section to jump-start an introduction/set-up/getting started section in your user guide. This document should remain as an appendix to your main report.
crumbs: Appendices
C  Task Analysis

title: User Guide: Annual Review Article Topic
text:
Introduction
Elo ratings are a popular way for ranking competitors in head-to-head competitions. Each competitor has a certain rating that updates after each and every match depending on the outcome. Rating differences can be used to predict future match results too. This guide explains how to implement an Elo rating system in R using the PlayerRatings package. It also includes instructions for loading and preparing match data, calculating Elo ratings, visualizing ratings and interpreting the results. The workflow is based on the concepts described by Glickman and Jones.(Glickman and Jones 2024)


Prerequisites
Before beginning this guide, users should have:

R and RStudio installed

Basic familiarity with R (loading data and using packages)

A dataset containing player1, player2, and result columns

Match data arranged in chronological order

Ensuring these requirements are met will allow the Elo rating system to function correctly.


Step 1: Data Preparation
Before model implementation, the dataset must be cleaned and formatted properly. Player names should be consistent across all observations, and matches must be ordered chronologically to ensure accurate rating updates.

library(dplyr)

# Placeholder dataset for demonstration
elo_data <- data.frame(
  player1 = c("Player A", "Player B", "Player A", "Player C"),
  player2 = c("Player B", "Player C", "Player C", "Player D"),
  result  = c("player1", "player2", "player1", "player2")
)

# Encode results numerically for Elo
elo_data <- elo_data %>%
  mutate(score1 = ifelse(result == "player1", 1, 0),
         score2 = ifelse(result == "player2", 1, 0)) %>%
  select(player1, player2, score1, score2)

elo_data

   player1  player2 score1 score2
1 Player A Player B      1      0
2 Player B Player C      0      1
3 Player A Player C      1      0
4 Player C Player D      0      1








Note



The dataset above is a placeholder created directly in R for demonstration purposes. In practice, users should replace it with their own match data containing the columns player1, player2, and result.




Step 2: Loading Required Package
The Elo rating system is implemented using the PlayerRatings package (Stephenson and Sonas 2020). The PlayerRatings package provides functions to help dynamically update player ratings
library(PlayerRatings)


Step 3: Parameter Specification
The K-factor controls how much a player’s rating changes after each match. The higher the K-factor means the faster the ratings change. A lower K-factor makes ratings a lot more stable.
The choice of the K-factor number depends a lot on the amount of data available. If there is aren’t a lot of matches or the player is new then having a higher K-factor, like 32, is useful because it allows ratings to adjust quickly. Then on the other hand, if there is a more established player with a lot more matches, a lower K-factor, like 16-24, is better because ratings don’t change as quickly.
k_factor <- 32


Step 4: Model Estimation or Updating
The Elo model updates ratings after each match by comparing what actually happened to what was expected. If a player performs better then expected it goes up and if not it goes down.
This process happens one match at a time, so ratings change as more data is added. Because of this, the matches must be in chronological order to get correct results.


Step 5: Evaluation
After calculating the ratings, it is useful to evaluate and visualize the results. This helps identify which players are ranked higher and whether the ratings appear reasonable.
The figure below shows an example of Elo ratings for a set of players.

players <- c("Player A", "Player B", "Player C", "Player D")
ratings <- c(1600, 1500, 1450, 1300)

barplot(ratings,
        names.arg = players,
        xlab = "Players",
        ylab = "Elo Rating",
        main = "Example Elo Ratings")







Figure 1: Example Elo ratings for players





As shown in Figure 1, players with higher ratings are considered stronger. These visualizations can help check that the model results make sense and are easy to interpret.


Step 6: Interpretation
After calculating the Elo ratings, you can use them to tank players and also estimate changes of winning future matches.

Players with the same rating have about a 50% chance of winning.

A player with 200 points higher than their opponent would win about 75% of the time.

A player with 400 points higher than their opponent would win about 90% of the time.

Larger differences in ratings indicate a higher likelihood that the stronger player will win. You should also check that rating changes are reasonable and stable over time, especially for top players.


Common Questions
Why are ratings not changing much?
This may be due to a low K-factor.
Why do results look incorrect?
Ensure matches are in chronological order.
Can I include ties?
Yes, but they must be encoded correctly depending on the package.


Additional Resources
R Packages

“PlayerRatings”: Includes Elo, Glicko, and other rating systems; documentation shows how to prepare and update ratings. (Stephenson and Sonas 2020)
“BradleyTerry2”: Fits Bradley–Terry paired comparison models; includes examples and vignettes. (Turner and Firth 2025)
“Elo”: Focused specifically on Elo ratings; provides clear examples for calculating ratings from win/loss data. (Heinzen 2023)

Documentation and Vignettes

Help files in R (e.g., ?PlayerRatings)
CRAN documentation
Example workflows showing data preparation and model execution

Research Papers
Original papers on Elo and Glicko, referenced in Glickman and Jones (2024), explain the statistical theory behind the models and how ratings are updated and interpreted (Glickman and Jones 2024).


References




Glickman, Mark E., and Andrew C. Jones. 2024. “Models and Rating Systems for Head-to-Head Competition.” Annual Review of Statistics and Its Application 11: 1–22. https://doi.org/10.1146/annurev-statistics-040722-061813.


Heinzen, Ethan. 2023. “Elo: Ranking Teams by Elo Rating and Comparable Methods.” https://cran.r-project.org/package=elo.


Stephenson, Alec, and Jeff Sonas. 2020. “PlayerRatings: Dynamic Updating Methods for Player Ratings Estimation.” https://cran.r-project.org/package=PlayerRatings.


Turner, Heather, and David Firth. 2025. “BradleyTerry2: Bradley-Terry Models.” https://cran.r-project.org/package=BradleyTerry2.

title: 1  User Guide: Implementing an Elo Rating System in R
text:
Step 1: Install and Load Packages
Firstly, to calculate Elo ratings, we need to install and load the required packages. The “PlayerRatings” package provides functions for Elo calculations, while “dplyr” is helpful for preparing and manipulating the data.

install.packages(“PlayerRatings”)
install.packages(“dplyr”)
library(PlayerRatings)
library(dplyr)
crumbs: 1  User Guide: Implementing an Elo Rating System in R

title: 1  User Guide: Implementing an Elo Rating System in R
text:
Step 2: Load the Dataset
Next, we load the match results dataset into R. For my user guide I’m using a fake match.csv as a placeholder for what it should look like. The dataset should include columns player1, player2, and result indicating the winner of each match. If we wanted to check the first few rows to confirm that the data is correct we would do something like this:

matches <- read.csv(“matches.csv”)
head(matches)
crumbs: 1  User Guide: Implementing an Elo Rating System in R

title: 1  User Guide: Implementing an Elo Rating System in R
text:
Step 1: Data Preparation
Before running any model, the dataset must be cleaned and formatted. First, check that all competitor names are consistent and correct any typos. Ensure matches are in chronological order, which is particularly important for dynamic ratings like Elo. Next, encode match outcomes numerically: wins as 1 and losses as 0. (For the sake of the user guide I will have a fake placeholder csv called matches).
library(dplyr)

matches <- read.csv("matches.csv")  # Replace with your file path

elo_data <- matches %>%
  mutate(score1 = ifelse(result == "player1", 1, 0),
         score2 = ifelse(result == "player2", 1, 0)) %>%
  select(player1, player2, score1, score2)

head(elo_data)
crumbs: 1  User Guide: Implementing an Elo Rating System in R

title: 1  User Guide: Implementing an Elo Rating System in R
text:
1.1 




Glickman, Mark E., and Andrew C. Jones. 2024. “Models and Rating Systems for Head-to-Head Competition.” Annual Review of Statistics and Its Application 11: 1–22. https://doi.org/10.1146/annurev-statistics-040722-061813.
crumbs: 1  User Guide: Implementing an Elo Rating System in R

title: 1  User Guide: Implementing an Elo Rating System in R
text:
Step 2: Model Selection
Next we decide which rating model is appropriate for our data. For static models, like Bradley–Terry, ratings assume outcomes are independent and do not change over time. For dynamic models, like Elo or Glicko, ratings update after each match. In this guide, we use the Elo model for dynamic updating.
library(PlayerRatings)
crumbs: 1  User Guide: Implementing an Elo Rating System in R

title: 1  User Guide: Implementing an Elo Rating System in R
text:
Step 3: Parameter Specification
Now we select starting ratings for each player and choose tuning parameters. In Elo, the K-factor determines how much ratings change after each match. A higher K-factor makes ratings more sensitive to match results.
k_factor <- 32
crumbs: 1  User Guide: Implementing an Elo Rating System in R

title: 1  User Guide: Implementing an Elo Rating System in R
text:
Step 4: Model Estimation or Updating
After that we use the Elo model to calculate or update ratings based on match outcomes. Each match updates player ratings using the specified K-factor.
elo_ratings <- elo(elo_data, k = k_factor)
elo_ratings
crumbs: 1  User Guide: Implementing an Elo Rating System in R

title: 1  User Guide: Implementing an Elo Rating System in R
text:
Step 5: Evaluation
After we estimate ratings, we evaluate how well the model performs. This can include checking predictive accuracy on future matches. It could also include comparing results under different K-factors, or examining rating stability over time. Visualizing ratings helps identify inconsistencies.
ratings_df <- as.data.frame(elo_ratings$ratings) %>% arrange(desc(Rating))

barplot(ratings_df$Rating,
  names.arg = ratings_df$Player,
  las = 2,
  xlab = "Players",
  ylab = "Elo Rating",
  main = "Elo Ratings of Competitors",
  col = "steelblue")
crumbs: 1  User Guide: Implementing an Elo Rating System in R

title: 1  User Guide: Implementing an Elo Rating System in R
text:
Step 6: Interpretation
Finally, interpret the results of the ratings. Use them to rank the different competitors, estimate win probabilities for future matches, and evaluate uncertainty. Larger differences in Elo ratings indicate a higher likelihood that the stronger player will win. Also consider whether the rating changes are reasonable and stable across matches.

“BradleyTerry2”: Fits Bradley–Terry paired comparison models; includes examples and vignettes.
“elo”: Focused specifically on Elo ratings; provides clear examples for calculating ratings from win/loss data.
